caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
a: Existing workflow automation may mean part of the apparent opportunity is already captured.
rho: The industry source reports intended automation activity rather than realized AI labor substitution.
rho: Small operators may lack clean historical job-cost data needed for reliable deployment.
e: Eligibility is not directly observed at six digits.
e: The provided firm count is an ESTIMATE based on margin-bridged size classes and may include firms lacking transferable recurring operations.

s5: The survey records plans, not completed transfers, and includes transfers that may not convey external control.

q: Customer concentration and paper-price clauses can dominate retention firm by firm.
d5: Consumer sell-through does not translate one-for-one into US printing volume because inventories, returns, imports, and run lengths vary.
d5: The constant-quality adjustment is judgmental and the 2024-2025 trend is a short window.

o: Large publishers may internalize or vertically coordinate production without eliminating the physical work.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business job and contract revenue from publishers and institutions, generally quoted per run or per unit with paper, setup, production, binding, and finishing embedded in the price.
lens.excluded_activities: Publishers, authors, retailers, captive in-plant operations, pure print-on-demand software platforms without accountable production operations, and firms outside the stated EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US book printers in the lower-middle-market band producing hardcover, paperback, educational, religious, professional, and other bound books for external publishers and institutional customers, including associated prepress, binding, finishing, and fulfillment performed with the print job.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 323 occupation mix and national printing occupations, not wage-weighted tasks inside book printers. The range maps AI-addressable estimating, scheduling, prepress file checks, customer service, sales administration, and inspection documentation onto a workforce dominated by physical press, binding, and finishing work; broader-subsector office shares could overstate book-printer exposure, while omission of embedded administrative work could understate it.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
Existing workflow automation may mean part of the apparent opportunity is already captured.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted employee roster and two-week task diary from at least ten independent book printers, split among prepress, estimating, customer service, planning, pressroom, bindery, warehouse, and management.
a.rationale: AI can remove work in quoting, job intake, imposition assistance, proof comparison, scheduling, purchasing, and routine customer communications, but cannot directly substitute most material handling, machine setup, press operation, binding, and maintenance. BLS shows printing press operators and binding/finishing workers as large, physically grounded occupations and the industry association identifies automation, business intelligence, and client web portals as productivity priorities.

d5.bridge: Circana BookScan unit sales at reporting retail outlets proxy physical book-printing demand, while AAP publishing revenue includes print and digital formats and does not measure domestic printer output. Near-flat recent print units are projected into a five-year constant-quality service ratio with downside for digital substitution and offshore sourcing; BookScan's incomplete channel coverage and omission of educational and institutional printing can bias either direction.
caveats: Consumer sell-through does not translate one-for-one into US printing volume because inventories, returns, imports, and run lengths vary.
The constant-quality adjustment is judgmental and the 2024-2025 trend is a short window.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year order volume, pages, impressions, and domestic sourcing data by trade, education, religious, and professional segment from Book Manufacturers' Institute members and major publisher procurement systems.
d5.rationale: US print book units rose slightly in both 2024 and 2025 and total publishing revenue grew in 2024, indicating a resilient physical format. Over five years, ebooks and audio, print-on-demand substitution, educational funding, import competition, title mix, and shorter runs create downside, while durable reader preference and more frequent short runs support a near-stable base.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly observed at six digits.
The provided firm count is an ESTIMATE based on margin-bridged size classes and may include firms lacking transferable recurring operations.
e.change_evidence: A screened census of the 81 estimated firms using customer concentration, repeat-revenue percentage, ownership independence, equipment condition, normalized EBITDA, and owner-dependence fields.
e.rationale: The frozen firm count is already bridged to the EBITDA band. Most independent book printers serve external publishers and receive repeat titles, reprints, seasonal educational work, or recurring institutional programs, but eligibility is reduced by customer concentration, owner-dependent selling, obsolete equipment, captive-like relationships, and plants whose economics depend on a single volatile title category.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 323117 ($790,853,843) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 323117 ($4,886,135,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $253,391 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 323117); class <5: 174 firms, avg revenue/firm $718,443 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $105,252 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 57 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,086,737 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $159,207 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,966,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $581,037 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,141,786 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,339,272 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,786,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,707,766 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $264,699,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,778,417 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 81

caveats: The distinction between an automated platform and its underlying accountable plant can be blurred.
Large publishers may internalize or vertically coordinate production without eliminating the physical work.
o.change_evidence: Publisher procurement interviews and printer order-level data measuring the share of volume migrated to publisher-owned plants, automated print-on-demand networks, offshore turnkey supply, or software-only self-service channels.
o.rationale: Physical books still require an accountable operator to procure paper, manage color and files, run and maintain equipment, bind, finish, inspect, pack, and meet delivery commitments. Self-service ordering and automated print-on-demand can reduce human touch and shift volume toward platforms, but they do not eliminate accountable physical production operations.

caveats: No book-printing contract sample or pass-through study was located.
Customer concentration and paper-price clauses can dominate retention firm by firm.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level gross-margin bridges for comparable reprint jobs before and after automation, including quote revisions, renewal dates, win rates, volume changes, and competitor responses across at least three annual buying cycles.
q.rationale: Quoted job and per-unit pricing can preserve some productivity gains until rebid or renewal, especially where reliability, color, binding quality, and turnaround matter. Sophisticated publishers compare bids, paper is transparent, and excess industry capacity encourages price competition, so a material portion of gains is likely shared through lower prices or service enhancements over five years.

caveats: The industry source reports intended automation activity rather than realized AI labor substitution.
Small operators may lack clean historical job-cost data needed for reliable deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor-hour, spoilage, and exception-rate data from book printers deploying AI-enabled estimating, preflight, scheduling, proofing, and customer-service tools for at least twelve months.
rho.rationale: Most exposed tasks use digital job tickets, PDFs, email, estimating data, and production schedules, making adoption feasible within five years. Implementation is constrained by fragmented MIS data, customer-specific specifications, low tolerance for costly print errors, cybersecurity, integration with legacy presses and bindery, and the need for human approval of color and quality decisions.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveyed US employer-business owners across industries and found 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Applying a discount for plans that do not consummate as qualifying control transfers yields the base; an older, capital-intensive printing owner base could make the all-industry proxy conservative, while family succession and closures could make it optimistic.
caveats: The Gallup population is not printing-specific and includes businesses far smaller and larger than the lens.
The survey records plans, not completed transfers, and includes transfers that may not convey external control.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed US book-printer transactions from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, printing-industry brokers, and lender records, matched to an ownership-age census of eligible firms.
s5.rationale: Printing is mature and fragmented, so retirements and consolidation should create transfer opportunities, but stated intentions exceed completed, financeable control transfers and some owner exits end in closure or equipment liquidation rather than a going-concern sale.

principal_driver: Repeat physical-book programs create durable workflows in which AI-enabled estimating, prepress, planning, and service can compound across many jobs.
principal_weakness: Most labor is tied to accountable physical production, while powerful publisher buyers can reprice away savings and source globally.
